title: Rewriting the Rules of Resolution: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (Amendment) Act, 2026 and What It Changes
description:
India's Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC), 2016, has received its most comprehensive overhaul with the 2026 Amendment Act. This landmark legislation addresses a decade of identified structural gaps, growing cross-border insolvency complexities, and the urgent need to resolve stalled real estate projects. Receiving Presidential assent on April 6, 2026, and largely commenced on May 26, 2026, the Act significantly reshapes India's insolvency framework.

Key amendments include the introduction of a new Creditor-Initiated Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). This mechanism enables notified financial institutions to initiate resolution for eligible corporate debtors without immediate Adjudicating Authority intervention, preserving a debtor-in-possession model for less complex entities. Critically, the Act empowers the Central Government to establish a statutory Group Insolvency framework for interconnected corporate entities and formulate cross-border insolvency rules, aligning India with international best practices.

Further reforms streamline liquidation processes through enhanced creditor supervision and clearer asset disposal guidelines. Procedural timelines for resolution and adjudication are tightened, and the Act clarifies avoidance transactions and fraudulent trading, expanding look-back periods. The regulatory and penalty architecture is reformed, largely shifting from criminal to civil penalties, and the "clean-slate" principle is strengthened by protecting licenses post-plan approval and extinguishing pre-approval claims, fostering investor confidence.

While the legislative architecture for new mechanisms like the Creditor-Initiated CIRP and Group Insolvency is now in place, their full operationalization awaits separate government notifications and subordinate legislation. This staged commencement reflects a deliberate strategy to build necessary regulatory and judicial infrastructure. The 2026 Amendment Act unequivocally signals India's commitment to an efficient, equitable, and globally aligned insolvency regime.

title: Down Syndrome and the National Trust Act: Kerala High Court Clarifies the Law on Legal Guardianship
description:
The Kerala High Court, in a significant judgment on June 3, 2026 (W.P.(C) No. 12482 of 2025), ruled that Down Syndrome constitutes "mental retardation" under the National Trust for the Welfare of Persons with Autism, Cerebral Palsy, Mental Retardation and Multiple Disabilities Act, 1999. This decision affirms a parent's right to seek legal guardianship for an adult child with Down Syndrome under the Act's protective framework, overturning an arbitrary administrative rejection.

The case stemmed from a father's application to be appointed as the legal guardian of his adult daughter, who was certified with Down Syndrome and 55% intellectual disability. The District Collector had summarily rejected the application, asserting that Down Syndrome was not a recognized disability under the Act. The petitioner argued that Down Syndrome, being a well-established cause of intellectual impairment, clearly falls within the definition of "mental retardation" as per Section 2(g) of the Act, and that the rejection was procedurally unsound due to lack of a hearing.

The Court's analysis meticulously examined statutory provisions and relied on authoritative medical literature, including the IAP Handbook and research reports from AIIMS, to establish that Down Syndrome inherently manifests as intellectual disability and sub-normality of intelligence, aligning with the Act's definition of "mental retardation." Furthermore, the Court found the District Collector's rejection to be procedurally flawed, having failed to comply with mandatory statutory requirements for scrutinizing applications, collecting materials, assessing the individual, and hearing all interested parties before making a decision.

Consequently, the High Court quashed the District Collector's rejection order and directed the reconsideration of the guardianship application within three months. This landmark judgment clarifies a crucial interpretive gap, providing much-needed legal recognition and support for families of persons with Down Syndrome, while simultaneously reinforcing the non-negotiable principle of procedural fairness in administrative proceedings concerning vulnerable individuals.

title: Unilateral Institutional Invocation: A Façade That Cannot Cure the Vice of Interested Party Appointment in Arbitration
description:
The Calcutta High Court, in L&T Finance Ltd. v. Jai Paras Material Store and Ors., affirmed that an interested party's unilateral invocation of an institutional mechanism cannot bypass statutory prohibitions against unilateral arbitrator appointments. Executing courts under Section 36 are empowered to refuse enforcement of awards stemming from such fundamental jurisdictional defects.

In this case, L&T Finance unilaterally referred a default dispute to Presolv360, an Online Dispute Resolution forum, for arbitrator appointment after the borrower's non-participation. An ex parte award was rendered, which L&T sought to enforce. The Court questioned the validity of an arbitration clause granting appointment power to an interested party, the legality of unilateral institutional invocation without consent, and the executing court's ability to address these fundamental issues.

The Court's analysis relied on Sections 12(5) and 14(1)(a) of the Arbitration Act, rendering interested parties ineligible and their mandates terminated. Citing Supreme Court precedents (TRF Ltd., Perkins Eastman), it underscored that an interested party cannot appoint an arbitrator, directly or indirectly, to ensure equal treatment (Section 18). Unilateral institutional invocation without mutual consent was deemed impermissible; Section 11 offers the exclusive remedy for neutral court appointment in non-participatory scenarios.

Crucially, the Court held that an award by a unilaterally appointed arbitrator is void ab initio, "non-est," lacking inherent jurisdiction. Such a fundamental defect, it affirmed, must be examined by an executing court under Section 36, irrespective of a Section 34 challenge, as no limitation cures a jurisdictional nullity. The execution petition was dismissed, the ex parte award declared unenforceable, with liberty for fresh, valid proceedings.

This judgment significantly reaffirms arbitral process integrity. It clarifies that executing courts must decline enforcement of awards suffering from such jurisdictional nullities, guiding financial institutions to utilize Section 11 court applications for non-participating respondents, rather than unilateral institutional invocation.

title: Procedural Non-Compliance and Limitation Defeat Bank’s SARFAESI Action: An Analysis of the Gopu Bala Reddy v. Union Bank of India Ruling
description:
The Debt Recovery Tribunal, Hyderabad, in `Gopu Bala Reddy v. Union Bank of India`, underscored the mandatory nature of procedural compliance and limitation periods under the SARFAESI Act. The ruling invalidated Union Bank of India's enforcement actions against guarantors for a loan, citing multiple legal breaches.

Crucially, the Tribunal found the bank failed to serve the principal borrower (M/s BRG Energy Limited, undergoing liquidation) with the mandatory Section 13(2) demand notice. This rendered subsequent measures void ab initio, emphasizing that borrower notification is essential even when pursuing guarantors.

Further procedural infirmities included non-compliance with Rule 8 of the Security Interest Rules for possession and sale notices. The bank published in an obscure newspaper, failed to provide proof of affixture for notices, and lacked valuation reports for the property, raising concerns of potential collusion due to the sole bidder.

Significantly, the Tribunal upheld the limitation defense. The loan was classified as NPA on December 31, 2011. The 12-year period under Article 62 of the Limitation Act for mortgage enforcement expired on December 31, 2023. Thus, the bank's fresh demand notice in March 2024 was time-barred under Section 36 of SARFAESI, rejecting COVID-19 exclusion arguments.

The DRT allowed the application, declaring the bank's measures void, setting aside the sale, and ordering a refund. This judgment is pivotal, reinforcing that SARFAESI powers are not unbridled and demand strict adherence to statutory procedures and limitation, profoundly influencing future enforcement strategies for secured creditors.

title: Dark Patterns in Ed-Tech: CCPA Imposes Penalty on PhysicsWallah
description:
India's Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) has levied a Rs. 5,00,000 penalty on ed-tech giant PhysicsWallah (PW) for deploying manipulative "dark patterns" on its platform. This landmark decision (Case No. CCPA-2/94/2025-CCPA, Order dated 1 June 2026) marks a significant enforcement of the 2023 Dark Patterns Guidelines, specifically addressing their application in the vulnerable ed-tech sector, which serves numerous minor students.

The CCPA identified three core violations. First, PW engaged in "Basket Sneaking" by automatically adding a pre-selected Rs. 10 donation to the 'PW Foundation' during checkout. Over 21 lakh users were affected, leading to approximately Rs. 2.47 crore collected. Second, "Confirm Shaming" was used, where descriptions of the foundation’s charitable work alongside the pre-selected donation emotionally pressured users to retain the charge. Third, PW committed "Forced Action" by mandatorily requiring mobile numbers and email addresses for "free" courses, despite no evidence of personalized service, thus subverting consumer choice.

Despite initial denials, PW later admitted the donation feature operated from February 2024 to December 2025. The CCPA emphasized that such practices violate the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, and E-Commerce Rules, particularly concerning vulnerable consumer groups like students. The order directs PW to cease these dark patterns, refund the collected donation amounts, and pay the penalty.

This ruling underscores that charitable elements in transactions must be genuine opt-ins, and "free" offers must disclose all access conditions upfront. It sets a crucial compliance benchmark for digital platforms, stressing that even individually small, systemically deployed manipulative practices affecting a large consumer base carry substantial regulatory risk, prioritizing consumer autonomy and informed consent over mere transparency.

title: Judicial Restraint under Section 34: Delhi High Court Upholds Arbitral Tribunal’s Refusal of Specific Performance
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Imran Ahmed Ansari & Anr. v. Intex Technologies (India) Ltd. & Anr., reaffirmed the narrow scope of judicial interference with arbitral awards under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The judgment guides on specific performance, patent illegality, and the limits of court intervention in arbitration. The dispute arose from an Agreement to Sell an industrial plot where the Arbitral Tribunal declined specific performance, instead directing sellers to refund ₹3.50 crores with 15% interest and reimburse transfer charges.

Aggrieved by the denial of specific performance, the purchasers challenged the award, arguing the Tribunal incorrectly applied Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, which presumes specific performance for immovable property. They contended their readiness to perform was ignored.

The High Court reiterated that Section 34 is not an appellate forum and prohibits re-appreciation of evidence or substitution of the court's view for the arbitrator's. It emphasized that an award can only be set aside for fundamental legal defects, such as patent illegality, not merely a different interpretation of facts or law. The Court found the Tribunal had consciously considered Section 10 and reasonably concluded that monetary compensation was adequate, given the transaction's commercial nature.

Upholding the award, the Court found the Tribunal's decision plausible and legally sustainable. It also affirmed the Tribunal's reasoning regarding the sellers' misrepresentation, the adequacy of reasons in the award, and the rejection of an alleged cash payment as being outside the arbitration scope. Dismissing the petition, the Delhi High Court underscored India's pro-arbitration stance, reinforcing minimal judicial intervention and the autonomy of the arbitral process, positioning Section 34 as a supervisory remedy rather than an appellate one.

title: Eureka Forbes Ltd. v. Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation: Reaffirming Fairness in Arbitral Proceedings
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Eureka Forbes Ltd. v. Indian Railway Catering and Tourism Corporation, reaffirmed that arbitral tribunals' procedural discretion must align with natural justice and fairness. The judgment critically examined the treatment of counterclaims when a party is proceeded against ex parte and clarified the distinction between a procedural order and an interim arbitral award.

The dispute arose from a Licence Agreement, leading to arbitration where Eureka Forbes filed a counterclaim. Following Eureka Forbes' non-appearance at two consecutive hearings, the Arbitrator proceeded ex parte against the company and rejected its counterclaim. He subsequently refused to restore the counterclaim, deeming its rejection a final determination beyond his jurisdiction.

Eureka Forbes challenged these orders under Section 34 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The High Court determined that the ex parte order was merely procedural and thus not challengeable as an interim award. However, applying a threefold test (final adjudication of a substantive claim, binding consequences, and the tribunal becoming functus officio regarding that issue), the Court concluded that the rejection of the counterclaim was an interim award. This rejection effectively brought a substantive claim to an end without adjudication on merits, violating statutory principles of fairness and equal treatment (Sections 18 and 25 of the Act).

The Court emphasized that mere non-appearance does not automatically justify dismissing an already filed counterclaim. Such claims should be adjudicated on available material, as procedural defaults should not lead to the forfeiture of substantive rights. Setting aside the interim award, the Delhi High Court restored the counterclaim for adjudication, thereby reinforcing that arbitration must uphold fairness, equality, and due process, ensuring meaningful access to justice.

title: Statutory Compliance Over Contractual Deviation: Telangana RERA Appellate Tribunal Upholds Penalty for Promoter’s Agreement Violation
description:
The Telangana RERA Appellate Tribunal, in Mehta & Modi Realty Kowkur LLP v. Deepa Suraj Premi, reinforced RERA's homebuyer protection mandate, upholding a penalty against a promoter for statutory violations and clarifying critical builder-buyer issues.

The dispute involved a promoter executing a sale agreement significantly deviating from the RERA-uploaded model (violating Rule 38). Homebuyers faced retrospective maintenance charges pre-possession via a prematurely formed, fraudulent "Greenwood Welfare Association." Other issues included demands for interest on delayed payments (during construction halts, on GST/TDS), and separate water charges, despite the project lacking an occupancy certificate.

The Regulatory Authority imposed a Rs. 10,99,992/- penalty for false information (Section 60 RERA). It directed maintenance payable only post-possession, dissolution of the existing association for a new one under the Telangana Cooperative Societies Act, and clarified GST applies solely to sale consideration, not interest.

The Appellate Tribunal affirmed this decision. It ruled that the agreement deviation violated Rule 38, justifying the penalty. Maintenance charges are statutory liabilities only post-possession, overriding non-compliant contractual terms. The Tribunal mandated association formation under the Telangana Cooperative Societies Act post-occupancy certificate and 60% allottee possession, upholding the existing entity's dissolution. While allowing water charges (utility security deposits), it disallowed interest on delayed payments due to incompleteness and clarified GST's scope.

This judgment underscores the supremacy of statutory compliance, dismissing the promoter's appeal and upholding homebuyer protection against RERA mandate circumvention.

title: The Last Word Belongs to the Deceased: Kerala High Court on Body Donation Rights
description:
The Kerala High Court, in Greeny Tomy & Ors. v. State of Kerala & Ors., decisively affirmed the primacy of an individual's right to determine the disposition of their body after death, even over the objections of surviving family members. The case addressed a fundamental question of posthumous autonomy, ruling that a deceased person's clear wish to donate their body for medical education must be respected. This judgment underscores the extension of dignity and bodily integrity beyond life.

The dispute arose when some children of a deceased woman, Mary, sought to reclaim her body for traditional burial, alleging they were not informed of her decision to donate it. However, other family members presented a written consent executed by Mary under Section 4A of the Kerala Anatomy Act, 1957, unequivocally expressing her desire for body donation. The legal issue before the Court was whether family members' preferences could override such a documented declaration.

The Division Bench emphasized that while legal personality ordinarily ceases upon death, the law protects certain interests of the deceased, including their body. It held that a person's decision regarding their body's treatment after death is an integral aspect of their autonomy and dignity. Since the authenticity and voluntary nature of Mary's consent were undisputed, the Court found no legal basis to disregard her wishes. Familial preferences, while emotionally significant, could not supersede a valid and clearly expressed decision made by the deceased during her lifetime.

Ultimately, the Kerala High Court upheld the initial ruling, dismissing the appeal. The decision reinforces that an individual's informed choice regarding body donation is lawful and must be honoured, strengthening public confidence in donation programs and balancing private sentiments with the public interest in medical advancement. It sets a significant precedent for upholding self-determination beyond death.

title: Writ or Tribunal? The SARFAESI Crossroads for Auction Purchasers
description:
The Punjab and Haryana High Court, in Devi Hospitality Pvt. Ltd. v. Punjab National Bank & Anr., has definitively ruled that the Debt Recovery Tribunal (DRT) under Section 17(1) of the Securitisation and Reconstruction of Financial Assets and Enforcement of Security Interest Act, 2002 (SARFAESI Act), is the exclusive and efficacious forum for aggrieved auction purchasers, to the exclusion of writ jurisdiction under Article 226 of the Constitution of India.

The petitioner, Devi Hospitality, emerged as the highest bidder in a PNB e-auction but encountered issues with the "Sale Confirmation Letter," citing deficiencies and subsequent allegations of fraudulent documentation by the bank. Despite depositing 25% of the bid amount, the petitioner sought an extension for the balance payment due to the COVID-19 pandemic. PNB, however, cancelled the sale and forfeited the Rs. 1.07 crore deposit, leading the petitioner to file a writ petition.

PNB contended that the writ petition was not maintainable, citing Supreme Court precedents like Agarwal Tracom Pvt. Ltd. v. Punjab National Bank. This case established that the forfeiture of an auction purchaser's deposit under SARFAESI Rules is an integral measure under Section 13(4) of the Act, challengeable solely before the DRT. The High Court, observing factual similarities to Agarwal Tracom, declined to adjudicate disputed facts in writ jurisdiction. It disposed of the petition, directing Devi Hospitality to approach the jurisdictional DRT within 45 days. The DRT was instructed to consider the application on its merits without dismissing it on grounds of limitation.

This judgment reinforces that the DRT is the designated and exclusive forum for challenging actions taken under the SARFAESI Act, including the forfeiture of deposits. It cautions against invoking extraordinary writ jurisdiction without first exhausting the available statutory remedies, which can lead to procedural delays and complications.

title: Section 29A Extension Maintainable Even After Mandate Termination, Parties Not to Blame for Arbitrator’s Failure to Pronounce Award: Himachal Pradesh High Court
description:
The Himachal Pradesh High Court, in Ramesh Chand v. National Highways Authority of India, has affirmed that applications filed under Section 29A(5) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, seeking an extension of time to conclude arbitration proceedings, are maintainable even after the arbitral tribunal's mandate has terminated by operation of law. The Court subsequently extended the time for the Arbitrator to conclude the proceedings until July 20, 2026.

The case originated from a land acquisition where the applicants, dissatisfied with an award, approached an Arbitrator. Arguments were heard and reserved, but the Arbitrator was transferred without pronouncing the award. The successor Arbitrator subsequently closed the proceedings, noting that the mandate had terminated under Section 29A(4) of the Act, granting liberty to parties to seek court intervention. The applicants then filed the present applications before the High Court.

The Court, relying on the Supreme Court's decision in Rohan Builders (India) Private Limited v. Berger Paints India Limited, reiterated that applications for extension under Section 29A(4) read with 29A(5) are maintainable even after the expiry of the statutory period. Finding that neither party was at fault for the Arbitrator's failure to pronounce the award post-reservation, and attributing the delay to administrative reasons, the Court determined there was sufficient cause for granting the extension. This ensures parties are not penalized for circumstances beyond their control.

title: Acquire the Land, Pay the Price: Madhya Pradesh High Court Reaffirms Timely Compensation as a Constitutional Right
description:
The Madhya Pradesh High Court, in a significant ruling in Shanti Singh & Others v. State of Madhya Pradesh & Others, reaffirmed that the right to receive compensation for acquired land is an essential component of the constitutional guarantee under Article 300-A. The Court emphasized that once land acquisition is completed and compensation duly determined, governmental authorities cannot indefinitely withhold payment on the pretext of procedural or administrative delays.

The dispute involved land acquired for a National Highway, where compensation of ₹3,35,40,000/- was awarded but remained unpaid despite the land being utilized. The Court rejected the State's argument of pending administrative formalities, asserting that bureaucratic delays cannot justify depriving landowners of compensation that has been adjudicated. It highlighted that arbitrary delays violate both Article 300-A and Article 14, undermining the rule of law and the integrity of the acquisition process.

The judgment underscores that fair and timely compensation is not merely a statutory benefit but an indispensable component of lawful acquisition, especially for policies like "missing plots" acquired through mutual consent. The High Court directed immediate disbursement of the compensation, preferably within eight weeks. This ruling reinforces constitutional safeguards, ensuring that developmental objectives do not override individual property rights and demanding greater administrative accountability in land acquisition processes.

title: Power of Attorney Holders and Cheque Dishonour Complaints: Gujarat High Court Settles the Scope
description:
The Gujarat High Court has clarified the validity of complaints filed by Power of Attorney (PoA) holders under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, especially following the enactment of the Bharatiya Nagrik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS). In United Petrofer Ltd. v. State of Gujarat, the court dismissed a petition seeking to quash a cheque dishonour complaint, addressing two primary contentions from the accused: the PoA holder's lack of valid authority and the trial court's alleged failure to hear the accused before taking cognizance, a requirement purportedly introduced by BNSS Section 223(1).

Justice L.S. Pirzada affirmed the PoA holder's competence, citing Supreme Court precedents. The Court reiterated that a proprietary concern can initiate legal proceedings through a duly authorised PoA holder, provided the agency relationship is disclosed and documented within the complaint. Regarding the procedural argument, the Court firmly held that the NI Act is a self-contained code. Its Section 142, through its "notwithstanding" clause, explicitly overrides general criminal procedure provisions, including BNSS Section 223(1). Consequently, magistrates are not obligated to issue notice or hear the accused before taking cognizance in Section 138 NI Act cases.

This ruling clarifies that proprietary businesses can effectively pursue NI Act complaints via PoA holders, emphasizing the need for clear documentation of authority. It decisively rejects the argument that the BNSS alters the NI Act's special cognizance regime, ensuring the continued efficacy of the NI Act as a recovery tool. The decision also underscores the importance of filing quashing petitions at the earliest stage, as the likelihood of obtaining such relief diminishes significantly once trial proceedings are advanced.

title: Jurisdictional Prerequisites Under Section 49 of the Water Act: The Rajasthan High Court on Valid Authorization for Criminal Complaints
description:
The Rajasthan High Court, in Pramod Jain @ Pramod Kumar v. The Regional Officer, Rajasthan State Pollution Control Board, Pali and Anr., has clarified the mandatory prerequisites for taking cognizance of offences under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. The Court reaffirmed that Section 49 of the Act constitutes a jurisdictional bar, requiring valid and specific authorization for a complainant to institute criminal proceedings. Without this, a criminal court is rendered defunct to entertain the prosecution.

The petitioner challenged a Sessions Court's remand order, arguing that the original criminal complaint lacked proper authorization from the Rajasthan State Pollution Control Board, rendering the entire proceeding non-maintainable. The respondent contended the remand was appropriate for fresh consideration.

The High Court emphasized the mandatory nature of Section 49. It distinguished between general powers of inspection (Sections 41 & 42) and the specific empowerment necessary for initiating criminal prosecution. The Court held that valid and legally sustainable authorization must exist in the complainant's favour at the time of institution of the complaint. Its absence triggers the bar under Section 49, making the complaint non-maintainable and stripping the court of jurisdiction.

The Court directed the Magistrate to first record a specific finding on the complainant's competence and lawful authorization, issued by the Board or a competent officer under relevant sections (e.g., 24, 25, 26, 43, 44, 47), before proceeding further. It also prohibited coercive measures against the petitioner until this foundational issue is resolved. This judgment underscores Section 49 as a crucial jurisdictional precondition, ensuring environmental prosecutions are based on demonstrable, lawful authorization.

title: From Conviction to Compromise: Supreme Court Opens the Door to Post-Sentence Settlements
description:
The Supreme Court of India has affirmed that an offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, for cheque dishonour, can be compounded even after conviction has attained finality and the accused has commenced serving the sentence. In its decision in Parsharvanath Weld Wires Pvt. Ltd. & Anr. v. State of Chhattisgarh & Anr., the Court underscored the settlement-oriented philosophy underlying such litigation, prioritizing substantive justice (full compensation to the complainant) over procedural finality.

The case involved an accused whose conviction under Section 138 was upheld by all appellate forums, leading to imprisonment. Shortly after the sentence commenced, the parties reached an amicable financial settlement. However, lower courts and the High Court rejected their request for compounding the offence, citing that the judgment had attained finality.

Overturning these decisions, the Supreme Court relied on Section 147 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, which unequivocally states that all offences under the Act are compoundable, overriding provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The Court emphasized that Section 138 proceedings, though criminal in form, are fundamentally compensatory. Once the complainant's financial claim is fully satisfied, the legislative intent favors restitution over punitive sanctions.

This landmark ruling clarifies that the power to compound extends to advanced stages, even post-sentence commencement. It reinforces that criminal proceedings arising from commercial transactions should not be pursued merely as a punishment once the underlying financial dispute has been resolved. The decision promotes restorative justice, enhances commercial efficacy, and contributes to judicial economy by encouraging dispute resolution and reducing the burden on the legal system.

title: Non-Production of Authorization Letter as a Curable Defect: Examining Judicial Interference in Secured Asset Recovery
description:
The Chhattisgarh High Court, in Central Bank of India v. State of Chhattisgarh, addressed whether the non-production of an authorization letter by an Authorized Officer under Section 14 of the SARFAESI Act constitutes a fatal or curable defect. The Central Bank of India sought assistance from the Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) to take possession of secured assets from a defaulting borrower. The CJM dismissed the application solely due to the absence of the authorization letter, prompting the bank to file a writ petition.

The High Court held that the omission of the authorization letter was merely a curable procedural defect, not a substantive infirmity. It emphasized that a Magistrate's function under Section 14 is purely ministerial, limited to providing assistance, and not adjudicatory. Therefore, a mechanical rejection without affording the secured creditor an opportunity to rectify such a lapse contravenes principles of natural justice and is impermissible. The Court set aside the CJM's order, directing reconsideration of the application after the bank submits the requisite authorization.

This judgment underscores that procedural formalities, while important, must not impede the substantive goals of the SARFAESI Act. It reinforces the delicate balance between efficient debt recovery and procedural fairness, ensuring that curable technicalities do not become insurmountable barriers to justice. The ruling clarifies the ministerial scope of Magistrates under Section 14, cautioning against their assumption of adjudicatory powers based on rectifiable defects.

title: Continuity in Coverage: Analysis of S.O. 2702(E) Notifying the Wage Ceiling for Mandatory Provident Fund Benefits under the Code on Social Security, 2020
description:
The Ministry of Labour and Employment has issued Notification S.O. 2702(E) on May 29, 2026, a critical step in operationalizing the Code on Social Security, 2020. This Code consolidates India's diverse social security legislation, including provident fund provisions. The notification specifies Rupees fifteen thousand per month as the wage ceiling for mandatory provident fund benefits under Chapter III of the Code.

Promulgated under the authority of Section 2(89) of the Code, this notification ensures regulatory continuity by preserving the existing wage threshold of ₹15,000 per month, which was last set in August 2014 under the erstwhile Employees’ Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952. This continuity is vital for a smooth transition to the unified statutory framework.

The established wage ceiling carries significant implications: employees earning up to ₹15,000 per month are mandatorily covered, obligating employers to fulfill provident fund contributions and related administrative responsibilities. Employees whose wages exceed this limit may opt for coverage on a voluntary basis, subject to mutual agreement. This notification is indispensable for providing clarity to the Employees’ Provident Fund Organisation, employers, and employees, thereby ensuring the seamless functioning of the consolidated social security architecture without transitional disruption.

title: No Coparcenary, No Karta: Supreme Court Rewrites the Conversation on Inherited Property
description:
The Supreme Court in Darubai & Anr. v. Kamalabai & Ors. reaffirmed a foundational principle: property inherited by Class I heirs under Section 8 of the Hindu Succession Act, 1956 (HSA) devolves as separate property, held as tenants-in-common, not as joint family or coparcenary property. Consequently, no heir can assume the role of 'karta' or unilaterally alienate the entire estate based on 'legal necessity' beyond their defined share.

This ruling stemmed from a five-decade dispute involving a widow who alienated inherited land, asserting karta authority. The SC, analyzing Sections 8, 10, and 19 of the HSA, clarified that statutory succession creates distinct, ascertainable shares, moving away from traditional Mitakshara survivorship. Each heir acquires an independent interest, making collective management doctrines inapplicable unless a coparcenary is otherwise proven.

The decision strengthens the HSA's statutory scheme, ensuring individual ownership rights override customary concepts inconsistent with it. It provides crucial guidance for property disputes, reiterating that property originating from a common ancestor does not automatically become joint family property after 1956 statutory succession. This promotes certainty in property relations and emphasizes due diligence in transactions involving inherited assets.

title: Dignity in Custody: Rajasthan High Court Condemns Custodial Humiliation as Unlawful Extrajudicial Punishment
description:
In a landmark ruling, the Rajasthan High Court, in Puranmal v. State of Rajasthan & Ors. (27 May 2026), unequivocally condemned the forcible public parading of an accused, cross-dressing, and the subsequent circulation of their photographs on social media as an unlawful form of extrajudicial punishment. The petitioner alleged that police officials subjected him to forced head-shaving, dressed him in women's clothing, and paraded him through a marketplace before his formal court production, causing grave and irreparable harm to his dignity and fundamental rights under Articles 14, 19, and 21 of the Constitution.

The Court rejected the police's explanation—that the accused was already in disguise and photos were taken by third parties—as inconsistent with ordinary human conduct and inherently implausible. Justice Farjand Ali underscored that such police conduct reflects a profound overreach of authority, infringing upon the core tenets of human dignity and constitutional morality. The judgment reaffirmed that the police, as an executive arm, are confined to law enforcement and public order, not adjudicating guilt or imposing punishments, which are exclusively judicial functions.

The High Court expressed deep concern over the increasing trend of "media trials by police," including "perp walks" and publicizing accused persons' images, which prematurely declare guilt and undermine the presumption of innocence. Citing Supreme Court precedents and the Law Commission's report on "Trial by Media," the Court emphasized that such actions inflict permanent digital scars, violating the right to live with dignity under Article 21. It clarified that Indian penal statutes exhaustively define lawful punishments, none of which include public shaming or media exposure; therefore, police-orchestrated humiliation constitutes an "extra-legal penalty."

To curb such practices, the Court issued general directions prohibiting police from compelling accused individuals to pose for media, publishing their photos/videos on social media or in the press, or making statements that insinuate guilt. It further mandated the prominent display of "Do’s and Don’ts" guidelines regarding custodial conduct and media interaction at all police stations and departmental web portals across Rajasthan. While refraining from immediate punitive action against the concerned officials, the Court reserved the petitioner's liberty to pursue legal remedies for damages, reinforcing the systemic importance of upholding custodial dignity and constitutional principles against police overreach.

title: Arbitration Through Conduct: Recognition of Arbitration Clauses Incorporated in Commercial Invoices
description:
The Allahabad High Court's decision in M/s Ganesha Ecosphere Limited v. Goodcore Spintex Pvt. Limited (2026) marks a significant step in Indian arbitration jurisprudence, affirming that arbitration clauses printed in commercial invoices can form a valid and enforceable agreement based on parties' conduct. This ruling addresses the reality of modern commercial transactions, which often rely on invoices, purchase orders, and actions rather than separately executed formal contracts.

In the dispute, the applicant supplied goods under invoices containing an arbitration clause. The respondent accepted the goods, made partial payments, and never raised objections to the invoice terms. However, when arbitration was invoked, the respondent argued against the existence of a formal written agreement. The Court held that the respondent's conduct—accepting goods, making payments, and silent acquiescence—collectively demonstrated mutual assent to the contractual terms, including the arbitration clause. The judgment heavily relied on Supreme Court precedents, such as Concrete Additives and Caravel Shipping, which recognized that an arbitration clause in invoices can be binding if acknowledged and acted upon, and that a formal signature is not always necessary if conduct implies acceptance.

The Court also reiterated the limited scope of judicial intervention under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, emphasizing that the inquiry is confined to determining the prima facie existence of an arbitration agreement, leaving substantive issues for the arbitral tribunal (competence-competence doctrine). Consequently, the Court found a prima facie arbitration agreement and appointed a sole arbitrator. This decision reinforces India's pro-arbitration stance, acknowledging commercial realities and strengthening the enforceability of arbitration agreements by recognizing consent inferred from parties' practical conduct.

title: The Right to Be Forgotten in Indian Law: A Landmark Framework
description:
The Delhi High Court, in W.P.(C) 1021/2016 (29 May 2026), has delivered India's first comprehensive framework for the "right to be forgotten," rooted in informational privacy under Article 21. Justice Sachin Datta's judgment distinguishes "open justice" (requiring accessible judicial records by case specifics) from "unlimited digital searchability" by name, which can disproportionately harm individuals.

The Court established de-indexing (removing from name-based search results) and masking (replacing names with neutral identifiers in public records) as reliefs. De-indexing uses a seven-factor proportionality test, favoring acquitted, discharged, or quashed cases, and matrimonial/private civil matters. Masking operates at the court record level, preserving full, unredacted versions for legitimate access.

Absolute bars apply: no relief for convictions related to offenses against women/children or breach of public trust. Public figures face stricter scrutiny for conduct in their public capacity.

Operational directions mandate Google and other search engines to de-index within two weeks, and Indian Kanoon to restrict name-based search functionality, also requiring it to implement systems for victim identity protection. These are deemed mandatory compliance under IT Rules 2021. MEITY is directed to ensure compliance.

This landmark decision clarifies that absence of specific legislation doesn't bar constitutional courts from enforcing this right. It carefully distinguishes de-indexing/masking from record erasure, ensuring records remain accessible by purposeful identifiers, upholding open justice while moderating algorithmic amplification. The framework balances privacy against public interest and accountability, setting a crucial precedent for digital rights in India.

title: Supreme Court Orders Time-Bound Removal of Forest Encroachments in Agasthyamalai Landscape
description:
The Supreme Court, in A. John Kennedy & Others v. State of Tamil Nadu, has issued a significant ruling mandating the time-bound removal of extensive forest encroachments within the Agasthyamalai landscape. This region, encompassing the Kalakad-Mundanthurai and Srivilliputhur-Megamalai Tiger Reserves and Kanyakumari Wildlife Sanctuary, is a globally recognized biodiversity hotspot. The Court observed decades of administrative inaction, inadequate enforcement, and widespread illegal occupation, including by serving and retired government officials, despite the State's partial rehabilitation efforts.

The judgment firmly reiterates environmental protection as a constitutional imperative, deriving from Articles 21, 48A, and 51A(g), and adopts an ecocentric approach, acknowledging the intrinsic value of nature. While stressing the necessity of rehabilitation for affected occupants, the Court clarified that humanitarian considerations cannot indefinitely defer the removal of encroachments from ecologically sensitive areas. It emphasized that conservation and rehabilitation are complementary, not mutually exclusive, objectives.

To ensure effective restoration, the Supreme Court issued comprehensive directives including a six-month deadline for the eviction of ineligible encroachers, a one-year timeframe for addressing issues related to the Bombay Burmah Trading Corporation Limited (BBTCL) leased lands, and the completion of surveys, demarcation, and rehabilitation schemes within specific periods. It also mandated disciplinary and legal action against implicated public officials and ordered continued judicial supervision through periodic status reports. This landmark decision reinforces institutional accountability, sustainable governance, and the judiciary's commitment to preserving critical ecosystems.

title: Arbitration Cannot Be Founded on Unilateral Acknowledgement: Delhi High Court Reaffirms Consent as the Basis of Arbitral Jurisdiction
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Matsya Fincap Pvt. Ltd. v. Govind Lal, ruled that a valid arbitration agreement, foundational to arbitral jurisdiction under Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, cannot be based solely on a unilateral acknowledgement letter. Such a document, signed by only one party and lacking contemporaneous evidence of mutual assent, is insufficient. Consequently, an ex-parte award based on it was deemed a nullity, incapable of enforcement. This judgment is crucial for finance companies and commercial entities, underscoring that consent forms the very source of an arbitrator's authority.

In the case, Matsya Fincap sought to execute an ex-parte award against Govind Lal, which was founded on a receipt-cum-acknowledgement letter. This letter, containing an arbitration clause and naming a specific arbitrator, was admittedly signed only by Govind Lal. The High Court emphasized that Section 7 mandates both a written agreement and consensus ad idem (mutual assent). It found the letter failed Section 7(4)(a) as it wasn't signed by both parties, and Section 7(4)(b) as no mutual communications evidencing agreement were produced. The mere presence of an arbitration clause in a unilaterally signed document is inadequate to establish a valid arbitration agreement.

The Court further held that the acknowledgement letter did not constitute a concluded, enforceable contract, lacking bilateral assent and essential contractual elements. A crucial aspect was the arbitrator's erroneous factual premise that the document bore both parties' signatures, which critically invalidated the arbitral jurisdiction. The High Court clarified that an executing court can refuse to enforce an award that is void ab initio due to inherent lack of jurisdiction, rejecting the appellant's argument for Section 34 challenge exclusivity. Concerns about the unilateral appointment of the named arbitrator, impacting fairness and impartiality, were also noted.

Ultimately, the Delhi High Court upheld the executing court's decision, dismissing the appeal and confirming the ex-parte award's nullity. This ruling serves as a vital reminder that arbitration demands clear, mutual consent, substantiated either by bilateral signatures or verifiable contemporaneous communications. It reinforces that arbitration derives its legitimacy from consent, mutuality, and fairness, without which an arbitral award is unenforceable.

title: Section 29A Inapplicable Where Arbitration Invoked Prior To 2015 Amendment: Delhi High Court In Partnership Property Dispute
description:
The Delhi High Court, in Prem Lata Surekha v. Chakradhari Surekha, affirmed an arbitral award in a significant family partnership dispute, underscoring the limited scope of judicial interference in arbitration matters. The dispute originated from M/s Sri Narayan Rajkumar, a firm constituted in 1973 with a 1974 partnership deed containing an arbitration clause.

Following a partner's death in 2003, Chakradhari Surekha alleged exclusion from the firm and claimed Prem Lata Surekha had fabricated subsequent partnership deeds to unlawfully transfer a valuable industrial property. Prem Lata countered that Chakradhari had retired decades ago, and the original 1974 deed was no longer valid.

In 2015, Chakradhari invoked arbitration. The arbitral tribunal meticulously examined evidence and found Prem Lata's alleged subsequent deeds unproven, relying only on photocopies and conflicting official records. Concluding that Chakradhari's retirement was not established, the tribunal upheld the 1974 deed as valid and directed an equal division of the property between the two surviving partners.

The Single Judge of the Delhi High Court upheld this award. On appeal, the Division Bench rejected arguments that Section 29A of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act (introduced by the 2015 amendment, prescribing timelines for awards) applied retrospectively, as the arbitration was invoked before its enactment. The Court also noted that other stakeholders had either settled their claims or voluntarily withdrawn. Reiterating the principle that Sections 34 and 37 do not permit re-appreciation of evidence, the Division Bench dismissed the appeal and imposed costs due to the appellant's non-bona fide conduct. This judgment reinforces the sanctity of arbitral findings, the necessity of strict proof for partnership changes, and clarifies the non-retrospective application of Section 29A.

title: CoC Autonomy Over CIRP Costs: NCLT Bengaluru on Interim Finance and Member Contribution
description:
The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) Bengaluru, in Gonugunta Murali v. Income Tax Department (15 April 2026), affirmed the Committee of Creditors' (CoC) paramount commercial wisdom regarding Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) funding, limiting judicial intervention. The Resolution Professional (RP) for Sai Krishna Minerals Pvt. Ltd. faced severe funding shortfalls for CIRP expenses. In its third meeting, the CoC, by a 67.25% majority, rejected direct member contributions and instead resolved to raise interim finance from external sources like banks or financial institutions.

Despite this CoC resolution, the approved interim finance did not materialize, forcing the RP to use personal funds. Consequently, the RP filed an application seeking NCLT direction to compel CoC members, including the Income Tax Department, to contribute proportionally. The Income Tax Department resisted, asserting it lacked statutory authority or budgetary provisions to fund private insolvency proceedings, and highlighted that the CoC had already approved an external financing model.

The NCLT, citing the Supreme Court's K. Sashidhar judgment, reiterated that the CoC's commercial wisdom is paramount and not amenable to judicial review. It held that the CoC's resolution to raise interim finance was valid and binding, exceeding the 66% approval threshold. The Tribunal also noted that compelling a Central Government Department to contribute lacked statutory basis and that the RP had not exhausted the CoC-approved alternative of seeking external finance. Therefore, the NCLT dismissed the RP's application, directing the RP to pursue the generation of interim finance from operational creditors, banks, or financial institutions as initially approved by the CoC. This ruling reinforces that judicial forums will not override the CoC's autonomous commercial decisions on CIRP funding, particularly when statutory creditors are involved and approved funding mechanisms remain unexplored.

title: Determination of Financial versus Operational Debt in Invoice Financing Transactions Under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016
description:
The NCLT Amaravati, in Kushal Finnovation Capital Pvt. Ltd. v. Rajesh Chillale, addressed critical questions regarding the classification of invoice financing as financial or operational debt and the validity of unregistered security interests during the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP). Kushal Finnovation Capital Private Limited provided "Invoice Financing–Dual Borrower" facilities to cotton suppliers, with Kallam Brothers Cottons Private Limited (Corporate Debtor) acting as guarantor.

Kushal Finnovation initially filed a claim for Rs. 5.33 Crore, admitted as an unsecured financial creditor. It subsequently sought reclassification as a secured financial creditor, relying on a general lien clause (Clause 5.1) in the credit agreements. However, the Interim Resolution Professional (IRP) rejected this request, asserting the debt was operational (stemming from bill discounting for goods purchased by the CD) and that no valid, registered security interest existed, especially considering prior registered charges held by other banks.

The Tribunal framed two pivotal issues. On debt classification, it determined that since Kushal Finnovation's recovery was confined to specific financed assets (cotton and related receivables), the facility was effectively "non-recourse." Consequently, it did not fall under Section 5(8)(e) of the IBC as financial debt. Instead, viewing its true commercial substance as the discounting of trade invoices, the Tribunal classified it as operational debt under Section 5(21). Regarding security interest, the Tribunal found Clause 5.1, a general lien over unspecified assets, insufficient to create an identifiable, enforceable charge without proper registration, which serves as crucial public notice.

Ultimately, the NCLT dismissed the application, deferring the reclassification decision to the IRP. This judgment reinforces that commercial substance takes precedence over contractual nomenclature. It clarifies that non-recourse invoice financing constitutes operational debt and that secured creditor status cannot be claimed through unregistered, unperfected, or broadly worded contractual liens. Financiers in the trade finance ecosystem must ensure security interests are properly documented and registered to secure their claims during corporate insolvency.

title: Qurban Without Cows: The Madras High Court Reaffirms the Absolute Bar on Cow Slaughter in Tamil Nadu
description:
In a significant pronouncement on 27 May 2026, the Madras High Court's Division Bench, comprising Justices G.R. Swaminathan and V. Lakshminarayanan, allowed a Public Interest Litigation directing the State of Tamil Nadu to ensure no cow or calf is slaughtered on Bakrid or any other day. The judgment, delivered in K. Surya alias K. Surya Prasanth v. Secretary to the Government & Ors., comprehensively reaffirms the absolute legal prohibition on bovine slaughter in the state.

The petitioner, K. Surya Prasanth, sought a Writ of Mandamus against arrangements for cow and calf sacrifices in temporary sheds for Bakrid. The Court anchored its decision in Article 48 of the Constitution, which directs the State to prohibit the slaughter of cows, calves, and other milch and draught cattle. Statutorily, it relied on Section 4 of the Tamil Nadu Animal Preservation Act, 1958, strictly mandating that an animal must be both over ten years of age AND unfit for work or breeding for slaughter to be permissible. Furthermore, G.O. Ms. No. 1715, banning cow and heifer slaughter in all slaughterhouses, was held to have the force of law.

The judgment drew upon established Supreme Court precedents, including Mohammed Hanif Quareshi v. State of Bihar (1958) and State of West Bengal v. Ashutosh Lahiri (1995), which clarified that cow sacrifice is not an obligatory or essential religious practice for Muslims. The Court also cited State of Gujarat v. Mirzapur Moti Kureshi Kassab Jamat (2005), which upheld the constitutional validity of a complete ban on the slaughter of the progeny of cows. Crucially, the Court emphasized that Rule 298 of the Tamil Nadu Urban Local Bodies Rules, 2023, mandates slaughter exclusively in designated and licensed slaughterhouses, expressly prohibiting temporary sheds.

The Madras High Court issued sweeping directives for the Chief Secretary and the Additional Director General of Police (Law and Order) to ensure statewide compliance, requiring a report by 29 May 2026. This landmark ruling reinforces the justiciability of Directive Principles, the binding nature of executive orders with legislative co-extensive power, and unequivocally establishes that no religious occasion can override clear statutory prohibitions, thereby upholding the supremacy of the rule of law.

title: The Delhi Fire Service (Amendment) Rules, 2025
description:
The Delhi Fire Service (Amendment) Rules, 2025, notified on May 26, 2026, usher in a transformative reform for the capital’s fire safety. These amendments introduce a technology-driven, decentralized compliance architecture via third-party auditors, automated monitoring, and administrative restructuring. Most provisions are immediate, but key changes for certification, auditors, and monitoring begin after ninety days.

The DFS is fundamentally restructured into five principal Fire Zones, thirteen Divisions, and thirty-nine Sub-Divisions, alongside a dedicated Fire Prevention Wing. Key definitions are updated, formally introducing "Fire Safety Auditor" as an empanelled third-party entity authorized to issue "Fire Safety Certificates" under delegated authority. Regulatory oversight expands significantly to cover more occupancies, e.g., residential over 15m, hotels over 12m, and various commercial, industrial, and hazardous buildings.

Empanelled Fire Safety Auditors (Rule 35) form the core reform. Owners engage auditors to inspect, report, and issue Certificates, which are delegated but subject to min. 5% random DFS verification. Owners retain primary responsibility; auditors are public servants bound by conflict-of-interest rules. A three-tier qualification framework, valid for three years, requires experience, indemnity insurance, mandatory training, and sets fee caps, with penalties for non-compliance.

An IoT-based Automated Continuous Monitoring System (ACMS) is mandated for expanded occupancies. Phase One monitors real-time active/passive measures with alerts; Phase Two integrates critical alarms with DFS. Regulatory processes are digitized, allowing online payments, clarifying compounding powers, and revising reward limits.

In essence, these amendments shift Delhi's fire safety to preventive, tech-enabled compliance. By decentralizing certification, mandating real-time monitoring, expanding regulatory scope, and embedding digital processes, they create a robust, accountable ecosystem for urban fire safety governance.

title: Regulatory Developments in Food Product Standards
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has published a draft notification dated May 21, 2026, proposing amendments to the Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011. Stakeholders are invited to submit objections or suggestions within 60 days, approximately until July 24, 2026.

The first key amendment introduces a new sub-regulation 2.2.10 for "Minor Seed Oils," including chilli, muskmelon, okra, and tomato seed oils. It establishes specific quality parameters for cold-pressed/virgin and refined oils, covering aspects like volatile matter, insoluble impurities, soap content, acid value, and heavy metals (iron, copper). Hexane-extracted oils must be refined, adhering to a 5.0 mg/kg hexane residue limit, while pressed oils must be hexane-free. Food additives are only permissible in refined minor seed oils.

Secondly, a new sub-regulation 2.3.66 defines "Edible seeds" for direct human consumption, encompassing both vegetable (e.g., watermelon, muskmelon, pumpkin) and oilseeds (e.g., sunflower, sesame, flaxseed). Proposed quality parameters include maximum limits for moisture (8.00%), free fatty acids (1.25% on dry mass), and foreign articles (0.1%).

Finally, the draft amends Appendix A, Table 14, to permit the use of potassium polyaspartate (INS No. 456) at a maximum level of 100 mg/kg in grape wines (Food Category System 14.2.3). This aligns the Indian framework with evolving international oenological practices.

These amendments aim to address existing regulatory gaps, enhance consumer protection, and provide greater clarity for food business operators.

title: FSSAI Notifies Amendment Regulations on Contaminants, Toxins and Residues: Expanded Scope for Pulse Flours, Seafood Residue Limits, and Arsenic Testing Protocols
description:
The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) has published the Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins and Residues) Amendment Regulations, 2026, effective December 1, 2026. Notified on May 14, 2026, these amendments update the 2011 regulations, following a public consultation without objections. The revisions aim to fortify food safety standards across three domains: metal contaminants, crop contaminants and naturally occurring toxic substances, and residues of veterinary drugs and antibiotics.

In metal contaminants, the maximum levels for lead and cadmium have been extended to "Pulses and Pulse flours," ensuring consistent regulatory scrutiny. A significant amendment for arsenic in fish oils introduces a maximum level of 0.1 mg/kg for inorganic arsenic (As-in) and a two-tier testing protocol. This requires initial total arsenic screening, with specific inorganic arsenic testing only if the total arsenic exceeds the permissible inorganic limit.

Amendments for crop contaminants refine product descriptions for total aflatoxins, clearly distinguishing between oils and ready-to-eat oilseeds. The saffrole limit of 10 is now explicitly applied to "Beverages and foods containing mace and/or nutmeg kernel as an ingredient," enhancing specificity. Furthermore, new residue limits address veterinary drugs in seafood: trimethoprim at 0.05 and oxolinic acid at 0.3 for "Sea foods including shrimps, prawns or any other variety of fish and fishery products."

The regulations provide a six-month transition period until December 1, 2026, enabling food business operators, testing laboratories, and enforcement agencies to align their protocols and product specifications. These comprehensive amendments represent a substantive advancement in India's food safety regime, aligning with evolving scientific understanding and global best practices.

title: Single Judge Designated as Commercial Court Cannot Be Treated as Lacking Jurisdiction Merely for Non-Transfer Under Section 15 of Commercial Courts Act: Supreme Court
description:
The Supreme Court has ruled that a judgment passed by a Single Judge, who was designated as a Commercial Court, cannot be deemed a nullity or without jurisdiction solely because a Section 34 Arbitration and Conciliation Act petition was not formally transferred under Section 15 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. This decision reversed a Calcutta High Court Division Bench order that had set aside a Single Judge's decision only on jurisdictional grounds, without examining the merits of the appeal.

The case originated from Steel Authority of India Ltd.'s (SAIL) challenge to an arbitration award via a Section 34 petition. During the petition's pendency, the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, came into force. The Single Judge subsequently dismissed SAIL's petition. The Division Bench later set aside this dismissal, arguing that the petition ought to have been transferred to the Commercial Division, thus rendering the Single Judge's order jurisdictionally flawed.

The Supreme Court, however, found that the Single Judge was, in fact, designated as a Commercial Court, as evidenced by the cause list. Crucially, SAIL had participated in the proceedings without objection and had not availed itself of Section 15(5) of the 2015 Act, which allows parties to seek transfer of such matters. The Court clarified that while a bench acting without proper assignment is a nullity, this principle did not apply here as the judge was appropriately designated. The lack of formal transfer was deemed a procedural or administrative irregularity, not an inherent jurisdictional defect.

Consequently, the Supreme Court quashed the Division Bench's order and remitted the matter to the Commercial Appellate Division of the Calcutta High Court to hear and decide the Section 37 appeal on its merits afresh.

title: Possession Without Protest Is No Waiver
description:
In a significant ruling on May 6, 2026, the Uttar Pradesh Real Estate Appellate Tribunal (UP REAT), in Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad v. Real Estate Regulatory Authority, Lucknow & Ors., affirmed that a promoter’s obligation to pay delay interest under Section 18(1) of the Real Estate (Regulation and Development) Act, 2016 (RERA) is absolute, mandatory, and cannot be waived by an allottee accepting possession without formal protest.

The case involved an allottee who received flat possession from the Uttar Pradesh Awas Vikas Parishad over three years beyond the stipulated completion date. After an Adjudicating Officer (AO) granted delay interest, the promoter appealed, contending the AO lacked jurisdiction for such claims and that the allottee, by accepting possession without protest, had implicitly waived their right to delay interest.

The Tribunal concurred that the AO lacked jurisdiction, setting aside the impugned order. However, it utilized its powers to provide complete justice, directly addressing the substantive issue of waiver. UP REAT unequivocally rejected the promoter's waiver argument, emphasizing that the "shall" in Section 18(1) makes the payment of delay interest a self-executing, absolute right for the allottee. Accepting possession, without more, does not extinguish this statutory entitlement, as doing so would undermine RERA's protective framework and incentivize promoters to delay.

Ultimately, the Tribunal directed the promoter to pay delay interest at MCLR+1% per month on the deposited amount for the delayed period (August 27, 2015, to August 31, 2017) within 45 days. This judgment significantly reinforces homebuyer rights, establishing that delay interest under RERA is a mandatory and mathematical entitlement, not a discretionary concession subject to implicit waiver through the acceptance of possession.
